cardinal fitness — results summaries

Statistical Methods

All analyses were performed in R (v4.5.2). Fecundity (egg counts) was modelled using a zero-inflated negative binomial mixed model fitted with glmmTMB, with cross direction and genetic line as fixed effects and replicate and plate well as nested random effects. Hatching rate was modelled using a binomial generalised linear mixed model (GLMM), also fitted with glmmTMB. Estimated marginal means and pairwise contrasts were obtained using emmeans. Post-blood-feeding survival, trans-heterozygote survival, and XA supplementation survival data were modelled using Weibull parametric survival models fitted with flexsurv. Midgut integrity (smurf assay) and adult eclosion data were modelled using binomial generalised linear models (GLMs) fitted with base R glm(). Gene expression data were analysed using the delta-delta-Ct method; ΔCt levels were modelled with a permutation-based linear model using emmeans for contrasts. Model tables were produced using sjPlot. Survival curves were visualised using Kaplan–Meier estimates alongside Weibull model predictions. All figures were produced using ggplot2 and patchwork.

Fecundity

Fecundity (egg count per well) was modelled using a zero-inflated negative binomial mixed model with cross direction and genetic line as fixed effects, and replicate and plate well as nested random effects (n = 341 observations across 340 wells, 10 replicates).

In the count component, both cd384R and cdg225 did not produce significantly different numbers of eggs (IRR = 1.09, 95%CI: 0.85-1.41, p = 0.49). For both lines where applicable the direction of cross was more important with the SDA x HET (1.31, [1.03-1.67], p = 0.029) and SDA x HOM (1.43, [1.09-1.88], p = 0.009) producing slightly more eggs than the inverse crosses.

The cdg384 line had the inverse of this response, with the SDA x HET (0.63, [0.47-1.86], p = 0.004) and the SDA x HOM (0.41, [0.28-0.59, p <0.001]) producing fewer eggs than the reciprocal crosses.

Fertility

Hatching rate (larvae per egg) was modelled using a binomial GLMM with a combined line × cross factor as the fixed effect, and nested random effects of plate well within replicate within line-cross group.

All comparisons are relative to WT. Most cross types within the cdg384 line did not differ significantly in hatching rate. A striking exception was the cdg384 SDA×HOM cross, which showed near-zero hatching relative to the reference (OR = 0.00, 95% CI: 0 - 0.01, p < 0.001), indicating that 1759 homozygous males are essentially sterile.

The single included cdg225 cross (SDA×HET) did differ significantly from the WT (OR = 0.15, p <0.001). In contrast, all tested cd384R crosses showed equal or higher hatching rates than the WT reference.

Survival after Blood Feeding

Post-blood-feeding survival was modelled using a Weibull parametric survival model with a single combined line × genotype factor (n = 384). Model estimates are scale factors relative to the reference group (cd225R Hom); values below 1 indicate shorter survival relative to this reference.

All KI heterozygote lines showed substantially reduced survival relative to cd225R Hom. The cdg338-384 Het and cdg225 Het groups had the most severe phenotypes (estimate = 0.07, 95% CI: 0.04–0.11, p < 0.001 and estimate = 0.09, 95% CI: 0.06–0.14, p < 0.001, respectively). The cdg384del Het group also showed markedly reduced survival (estimate = 0.20, 95% CI: 0.12–0.32, p < 0.001). cdg384 Het mosquitoes showed a non-significant trend toward longer survival than the reference (estimate = 1.49, 95% CI: 0.93–2.36, p = 0.095). Within the cdg384 line, homozygotes survived considerably less well than cd225R Hom (estimate = 0.40, 95% CI: 0.25–0.63, p < 0.001), whilst WT siblings survived longer (estimate = 2.03, 95% CI: 1.28–3.23, p = 0.003). WT survival did not differ significantly from the reference in the cdg384del line (estimate = 1.19, 95% CI: 0.75–1.90, p = 0.457), and the cdg338-384 WT estimate was highly unstable (estimate = 514758.94, 95% CI: 0.00–Inf, p = 0.996), consistent with near-complete survival in that group. cd225R WT mosquitoes did not differ significantly from cd225R Hom (estimate = 0.70, 95% CI: 0.44–1.11, p = 0.130). cdg225 WT mosquitoes survived substantially longer than the reference (estimate = 2.73, 95% CI: 1.71–4.34, p < 0.001).

`r2()` does not support models of class `flexsurvreg`.

TransHet survival

To assess whether the post-blood-feeding survival phenotype is caused by disruption of cardinal function, survival data were modelled for the 2360B5 and D251 lines specifically, using a Weibull parametric model (n = 186). Predictors were line (2360B5 vs D251) and genotype (Het, Hom, WT), with the reference group being 2360B5 Het.

D251 Het mosquitoes did not differ significantly from 2360B5 Het (estimate = 0.97, 95% CI: 0.40–2.34, p = 0.944). D251 WT mosquitoes showed dramatically extended survival relative to 2360B5 Het (estimate = 12218566.26, 95% CI: 0.00–Inf, p = 0.997), though the estimate is highly unstable, suggesting complete or near-complete separation in the data. Within the 2360B5 line, WT mosquitoes survived substantially longer than Het mosquitoes (estimate = 26.66, 95% CI: 11.04–64.38, p < 0.001). These results indicate that WT mosquitoes in both lines survive much longer than their KI heterozygote counterparts, but do not provide clear evidence that the D251 KO allele rescues the KI survival phenotype.

`r2()` does not support models of class `flexsurvreg`.

Smurf assay

Midgut integrity was assessed using the smurf assay. The proportion of smurf-positive mosquitoes (indicating gut barrier disruption) was modelled using a binomial GLM with genotype (WT, KI, KO) as the sole predictor, restricted to sucrose-delivered mosquitoes (n = 9 cage-level observations). The reference group was WT.

KI mosquitoes had a dramatically elevated smurf rate compared to WT controls (OR = 65.6, 95% CI: 25.7–205.2, p < 0.001), confirming that the cardinal KI allele causes significant gut barrier disruption after blood feeding. The KO genotype showed a smurf rate consistent with zero events (OR ≈ 0, p = 0.996), reflecting complete separation with no observed smurf-positive cases in this genotype. Taken together, the data confirm that the KI phenotype is associated with midgut barrier disruption, while the KO allele does not share this defect.

XA Supplementation

The effect of xanthurenic acid (XA) supplementation on post-blood-feeding survival was examined using a Weibull parametric survival model (n = 387). Predictors were supplementation route (blood vs sugar), XA dose (0 mM, 6 mM NaOH control [6mM C−], or 6 mM XA), and genotype (Het vs WT). The reference group was Het mosquitoes receiving blood supplementation at 0 mM.

Sugar supplementation was associated with substantially reduced time-to-event relative to blood supplementation (estimate = 0.50, 95% CI: 0.24–0.76, p < 0.001), indicating longer survival on the sugar route. WT mosquitoes survived considerably longer than Het mosquitoes (estimate = 1.89, 95% CI: 0.88–2.91, p < 0.001). The 6 mM NaOH control condition (6mM C−) did not significantly alter survival relative to 0 mM (estimate = −0.09, 95% CI: −0.37–0.19, p = 0.534). The 6 mM XA condition was associated with significantly longer survival compared to 0 mM (estimate = 0.50, 95% CI: 0.14–0.85, p = 0.006). These results indicate that 6 mM XA supplementation significantly extends post-blood-feeding survival in KI heterozygotes, and that this effect is not attributable to the NaOH vehicle.

`r2()` does not support models of class `flexsurvreg`.

Homozygous Survival

Adult eclosion was assessed from balanced crosses. The proportion of adults eclosing out of an expected 200 was modelled with a binomial GLM including line (1759 vs QA383P), genotype (HET, WT), sex, and all two- and three-way interactions (n = 16 observations; HOM observations excluded due to missingness). The reference cell was QA383P, HET, female.

The cdg384 line (1759) did not differ significantly from QA383P in HET female eclosion rate (OR = 1.22, p = 0.157). WT females did not differ significantly from HET females in either line (OR = 0.95, p = 0.722). Neither sex nor any two- or three-way interaction reached statistical significance (all p > 0.16). These results indicate that, in the absence of homozygotes, eclosion rates are broadly similar between lines, genotypes, and sexes, and that no strong line-by-genotype or line-by-sex effects on HET/WT viability are detectable in this dataset.
